Cookbook | Recipes | Ingredients | Equipment | Techniques | Cookbook Disambiguation Pages | Ingredients

**Croutons** are small chunks of dried bread that are toasted or baked until crisp.[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2)

## Characteristics

[edit | edit source]

The chunks of bread may be regular in shape (e.g. cuboid) or irregularly torn. Size-wise, they should ideally fit on a fork so that they are easily picked up as a part of a dish.[[3]](#cite_note-:0-3) They may be made from a variety of breads and seasoned with a variety of herbs and other flavorings, such as cheese, garlic, and tomato. Delicate breads, like brioche, will make delicate, powdery croutons, and vice versa.[[3]](#cite_note-:0-3) Sweet croutons are rare but not unheard of.[[3]](#cite_note-:0-3)

## Selection and storage

[edit | edit source]

Croutons may be purchased from a store or homemade, though the general consensus is that homemade croutons have better flavor.[[4]](#cite_note-:1-4) In terms of storage, croutons should be kept in an airtight container at room temperature. Croutons made with butter will go rancid sooner than those made with oil.[[3]](#cite_note-:0-3)

## Preparation

[edit | edit source]

Croutons are quite easy to make. Simply cut or tear your desired bread into bite-size pieces, toss with oil and seasonings as desired, and toast on a baking sheet at 350°F (180°C).[[3]](#cite_note-:0-3)[[4]](#cite_note-:1-4)[[5]](#cite_note-:2-5) You don't want the croutons to be fully dry all the way through while toasting, since they will harden once cooled.[[3]](#cite_note-:0-3) You can also make croutons in a skillet, but you have to pay more attention to them to prevent burning.[[3]](#cite_note-:0-3)[[4]](#cite_note-:1-4)

## Use

[edit | edit source]

Croutons are most commonly used to add texture to dishes like salads and soups.[[5]](#cite_note-:2-5) They can also be used to make stuffing or savory bread puddings.
